info.name: IMDB Click Race - Firebase API
info.description:
Practice collection that mirrors the Firebase Realtime Database calls made by content.js in the IMDB Click Race extension. Run the requests in folder order (1 -> 6) to simulate a game lifecycle: sign in, create a game, have a 'player' join, send a chat message, read it back, then clean up.

All requests use the {{db_url}}, {{firebase_api_key}} and {{id_token}} variables from the 'IMDB Click Race - Local' environment - select that environment before running anything.

request.description:
This is the same call getFirebaseToken() makes in content.js when there's no cached token yet. Firebase issues a fresh anonymous user + an idToken (used for the ?auth= param on every database call) and a refreshToken (so we don't have to sign in again every time).

The Tests tab below automatically saves both tokens into your environment so the rest of the requests in this collection 'just work'.

exec: // Save the tokens so every other request in this collection can use them

request.description:
This mirrors what createGameAndStart() does in content.js: a PUT writes a brand new game record at games/{gameId}, completely replacing whatever was there before (PUT = 'set this whole object').

Note: {{$timestamp}} is a Postman dynamic variable (current Unix time in seconds) - we multiply by 1000 to match the millisecond timestamps the extension uses (Date.now()).

This mirrors dbGet() in content.js - a plain GET returns the full JSON for that path. Run this any time after step 2 to see the current state of your test game, and again after steps 4/5 to see your changes land.

This mirrors the click-tracking dbPatch() call in content.js (the one that fires every time a player clicks an actor link). PATCH only updates the fields you send - everything else on the player record (like 'ready') is left untouched.

Try running step 3 before and after this request to see just the 'clicks' field change.

item.name: 5. Send Chat Message (PATCH with generated key)

request.description:
This mirrors sendChatMessage() in content.js. In the real extension, randId(10) generates a random key for each chat message so multiple messages don't overwrite each other. Here {{$randomAlphaNumeric}} (a Postman dynamic variable) does the same job - each click of Send produces a fresh random path.

After running this a few times, run step 3 (Read Game State) and look under the 'chat' object - you should see one entry per message, each with its own random key.

This mirrors dbDelete(), used by cleanupOldGames() to remove stale games after 24 hours. Run this last to remove the TESTPM01 game you created in step 2, so it doesn't clutter your real Firebase database.

After running this, step 3 (Read Game State) should return 'null'.

item.name: 8. SIMULATE: Add Fake Player to a REAL Game (PATCH)

request.description:
THIS IS THE FUN ONE. Use this to make a 'fake' second (or third) player join a REAL game you've created in your browser using the actual extension.

HOW TO USE:
1. In your browser, load the extension on IMDB and create a real game (as host). Open the lobby.
2. Find the real game ID - it's shown in the lobby UI, or you can check chrome.storage via the extension's debug tools.
3. In this Postman environment, set 'real_game_id' to that real game ID (click the eye icon next to the environment dropdown, or open the environment and edit the value directly).
4. Make sure you've run request 1 (Sign-In) so {{id_token}} is populated.
5. Click Send on THIS request.
6. Switch back to your browser - the lobby should update live (via the extension's SSE sync) to show 'Postman Bot' as a new player, with NO page reload needed.

Try changing 'fake_player_id' and 'fake_player_name' and sending again to add a 3rd, 4th player etc. - great for testing how your lobby UI handles multiple players without juggling Chrome profiles.

WARNING: this writes into a REAL game, so only do this on a test game you created yourself (not a real friend's game!), and remember to leave/delete the game afterwards to clean up.

item.name: 7. (Bonus) Try Without Auth Token - should fail

request.description:
This deliberately omits the ?auth={{id_token}} parameter. Your Firebase Security Rules require auth != null, so this should be rejected with 'Permission denied'.

This is a good one to run first (before step 1) and again after step 1, to see the same request behave differently depending on whether you're authenticated - a nice illustration of how the extension's auth layer protects the database.
